questions.stem: When cloning a database, which privileges are inherited by the clone of the database container itself?

options.text: All privileges granted on the source database
Only OWNERSHIP privilege on the source database
No privileges are inherited on the clone of the database container
Only SELECT privilege on the source database

explanation.correct: The clone of the container itself (database or schema) does not inherit any privileges granted on the source container.

distractors.A: Privileges on the source container are not inherited by the clone of the container; they are inherited only by clones of child objects.
distractors.B: OWNERSHIP is not inherited; the role that creates the clone may have ownership on pipes but not on the container clone.
distractors.D: SELECT is not automatically inherited on the container clone.

questions.stem: Which clause can be used with CREATE TABLE ... CLONE to copy all privileges except OWNERSHIP from the source table to the new table?

options.text: COPY GRANTS
COPY PRIVILEGES
INHERIT GRANTS
GRANT ALL

explanation.correct: The COPY GRANTS parameter in CREATE TABLE ... CLONE copies all privileges except OWNERSHIP from the source table to the new table.

distractors.B: There is no COPY PRIVILEGES clause for cloning; COPY GRANTS is the correct syntax.
distractors.C: INHERIT GRANTS is not a supported clause; privileges are not automatically inherited on clones.
distractors.D: GRANT ALL is used for granting privileges, not for copying during cloning.

questions.stem: When a table with a clustering key is cloned, what is the default state of Automatic Clustering for the new table?

options.text: Automatic Clustering is enabled by default
Automatic Clustering is suspended by default
Automatic Clustering is not supported on cloned tables
Automatic Clustering must be explicitly disabled after cloning

explanation.correct: When a table with a clustering key is cloned, Automatic Clustering is suspended for the new table by default.

distractors.A: This is the opposite of the documented behavior; it is suspended, not enabled.
distractors.C: Cloned tables can have clustering keys and support Automatic Clustering after resuming.
distractors.D: It is suspended, so you need to resume it if you want it enabled, not disable.

questions.stem: What is the restriction when cloning event tables?

options.text: Event tables can only be cloned within the same schema
You can clone a regular table to an event table
You can clone an event table only to another event table
Event tables cannot be cloned at all

explanation.correct: When cloning an event table, you can clone to and from only event tables; regular tables cannot be cloned to event tables or vice versa.

distractors.A: The restriction is about the type of table, not the schema location.
distractors.B: You cannot clone a regular table to an event table.
distractors.D: Event tables can be cloned, but only to other event tables.

questions.stem: If you clone a privacy-protected table only (not the schema), what happens to the privacy policy?

options.text: The privacy policy is cloned with the table
The privacy policy is not cloned
The privacy policy is cloned only if it is in the same database
The privacy policy is automatically associated with the cloned table

explanation.correct: When you clone only the privacy-protected table, the privacy policy is not cloned; it remains associated only with the source table.

distractors.A: The policy is not cloned when cloning only the table; cloning the schema containing both is required for the policy to be cloned.
distractors.C: Even if the policy is in the same database, it is not cloned when cloning only the table.
distractors.D: It is not automatically associated; the policy is not cloned.

questions.stem: Select TWO statements that correctly describe access control privileges when cloning objects.

options.text: If the source object is a database, the clone inherits all granted privileges on the clones of all child objects contained in the source database.
The clone of the container itself always inherits the privileges granted on the source container.
CREATE <object> ... CLONE statements always copy grants on the source object to the object clone.
The COPY GRANTS clause can be used with CREATE TABLE ... CLONE to copy all privileges except OWNERSHIP.
For pipes, the role that creates the clone has the OWNERSHIP privilege on the cloned pipes.

explanation.correct: A is correct: when cloning a database, all granted privileges on the clones of all child objects are inherited. E is correct: the role that creates the clone takes OWNERSHIP on cloned pipes.

distractors.B: The clone of the container itself does not inherit privileges from the source container.
distractors.C: For most objects, grants are not copied; only when COPY GRANTS is specified do they copy, and not always.
distractors.D: While COPY GRANTS does copy privileges except OWNERSHIP, it does not 'always' copy; it requires the parameter to be specified.

questions.stem: Select TWO correct statements about how foreign key constraints behave when cloning tables.

options.text: If the database containing both tables is cloned, the cloned foreign key references the cloned primary key table.
If the tables are in separate schemas, the cloned foreign key always references the cloned primary key table.
If the tables are in separate databases, the cloned foreign key references the primary key in the source table.
Foreign key constraints are never cloned; they must be recreated manually.
If the tables are in the same schema, the cloned foreign key references the source primary key.

explanation.correct: A is correct: when the database containing both tables is cloned, the cloned foreign key references the cloned primary key. C is correct: when tables are in separate databases, the cloned foreign key references the primary key in the source table.

distractors.B: If tables are in separate schemas, the behavior depends on whether the schema containing both is cloned; if not, it references the source.
distractors.D: Foreign key constraints are cloned and referenced appropriately; they are not recreated manually.
distractors.E: If tables are in the same schema and the schema is cloned, the cloned foreign key references the cloned primary key, not the source.

questions.stem: Select THREE correct statements about cloning stages.

options.text: External named stages can be cloned individually.
Cloning an external stage copies the data files from the referenced cloud storage.
When cloning a database, internal named stages are cloned only if the INCLUDE INTERNAL STAGES clause is used.
When cloning a schema, table stages associated with cloned tables are empty in the clone.
Internal named stages are always cloned when cloning a database or schema.
External named stages are not cloned when cloning a database or schema.

explanation.correct: A is correct: external named stages can be cloned individually. C is correct: internal named stages are cloned only when INCLUDE INTERNAL STAGES is used. D is correct: data files in table stages are not copied; cloned table stages are empty.

distractors.B: Cloning an external stage does not copy data files; it references the same cloud storage.
distractors.E: Internal named stages are not always cloned; they require the INCLUDE INTERNAL STAGES clause.
distractors.F: External named stages that were present in the source when cloning started are cloned, so they are not excluded.

questions.stem: Select TWO correct statements about cloning pipes.

options.text: Pipes that reference an internal stage are cloned when a database is cloned.
Pipes that reference an external stage are not cloned when a database is cloned.
A cloned pipe with AUTO_INGEST = TRUE is set to the STOPPED_CLONED state by default.
A cloned pipe with AUTO_INGEST = FALSE is paused by default.
The role that creates the clone does not have OWNERSHIP on the cloned pipe.

explanation.correct: C is correct: when AUTO_INGEST = TRUE, a cloned pipe is set to STOPPED_CLONED. D is correct: when AUTO_INGEST = FALSE, a cloned pipe is paused by default.

distractors.A: Pipes referencing internal stages are not cloned.
distractors.B: Pipes referencing external stages are cloned, so this statement is false.
distractors.E: The role that creates the clone takes OWNERSHIP on the cloned pipe.

questions.stem: Select TWO correct statements about tag-based masking policies when cloning a schema.

options.text: If the tag, masking policy, and table all exist in the cloned schema, the cloned table is protected by the policy in the cloned schema.

If the tag is in a different schema than the masking policy and table, cloning the schema containing the policy and table results in the cloned table being protected by the policy in the source schema.
If the tag is in a different schema, the cloned table is protected by the policy in the cloned schema.
When a table protected by a schema-level tag-based policy is cloned, it retains protection from the source schema-level policy.
If the tag is in the same schema, the cloned table is protected by the source policy, not the cloned policy.

explanation.correct: A is correct: when tag, policy, and table are in the same schema, the cloned table uses the cloned policy. B is correct: if the tag is in a different schema, the cloned table uses the source policy.

distractors.C: This is the opposite; when the tag is in a different schema, the cloned table does not use the cloned policy.
distractors.D: When cloned to a different schema, it uses the target schema's policy if available, not the source's.
distractors.E: If the tag is in the same schema, it uses the cloned policy, not the source.

questions.scenario: A database contains a table with a column that references a sequence for default values. The database also contains the sequence. An administrator clones the entire database. The cloned table needs to reference the cloned sequence.
questions.stem: Which statement correctly describes the behavior of the default sequence reference in the cloned table after the database clone?

options.text: The cloned table references the source sequence because sequences are never cloned.
The cloned table references the cloned sequence because both the table and sequence are in the cloned database.
The cloned table references the source sequence by default and must be manually altered to use the cloned sequence.
The cloned table references neither sequence and default values must be redefined.

explanation.correct: When the database or schema containing both the table and sequence is cloned, the cloned table references the cloned sequence automatically.

distractors.A: Sequences can be cloned when the containing database or schema is cloned.
distractors.C: The behavior is automatic; the cloned table references the cloned sequence, not the source sequence.
distractors.D: The reference is maintained; it points to the cloned sequence.

questions.scenario: A data engineer wants to clone an Apache Iceberg table that uses Snowflake-provided storage (EXTERNAL_VOLUME = 'SNOWFLAKE_MANAGED'). The source table is transient and the new table is also intended to be transient.
questions.stem: What is the outcome of the CREATE ICEBERG TABLE ... CLONE operation in this scenario?

options.text: The operation succeeds because both tables are transient.
The operation fails because transient tables cannot be cloned.
The operation succeeds only if the source table is permanent.
The operation fails because Iceberg tables do not support cloning.

explanation.correct: For Iceberg tables with Snowflake-managed storage, cloning succeeds only when the source and new tables are both transient or both permanent.

distractors.B: Transient tables can be cloned as long as the target is also transient.
distractors.C: The operation succeeds for both transient and permanent as long as they match; it does not require the source to be permanent.
distractors.D: Iceberg tables do support cloning under the specified conditions.

questions.scenario: A schema contains a table and a masking policy. The table has the masking policy applied. An administrator clones the schema.
questions.stem: What happens to the masking policy mapping on the cloned table?

options.text: The cloned table maps to the source policy, not the cloned policy.
The cloned table maps to the corresponding cloned policy in the target schema.
The masking policy is not cloned, and the cloned table has no policy.
The cloned table maps to both the source and cloned policies.

explanation.correct: When a table and its masking policy are in the same schema and that schema is cloned, the cloned table maps to the cloned policy in the target schema.

distractors.A: This happens only if the policy is in a different schema (foreign reference) or if the policy is not cloned.
distractors.C: Policies within the schema are cloned and remain attached.
distractors.D: It maps to only one policy, not both.

questions.scenario: A database clone operation is in progress. While the clone is running, a user drops a table named 't_sales' from the source database and then renames another table to 't_sales'.
questions.stem: What is the likely outcome of the cloning operation?

options.text: The clone completes successfully with the new 't_sales' table included.
The clone operation produces an error due to a name conflict.
The clone operation ignores the renamed table and proceeds.
The clone operation creates the table under a different name.

explanation.correct: DDL statements that rename objects during cloning can cause name conflicts, leading to an error.

distractors.A: Name conflicts can cause the clone to fail, not succeed.
distractors.C: The operation does not ignore it; it may conflict and fail.
distractors.D: Tables are not renamed automatically; an error occurs.

questions.scenario: A database db1 has a data retention period of 7 days. A table t1 in db1 has a data retention period of 1 day. A user attempts to clone db1 using Time Travel at a point 2 days in the past.
questions.stem: What is the result of the cloning operation?

options.text: The cloning operation succeeds and includes t1 as it existed 2 days ago.
The cloning operation fails because historical data for t1 is no longer available.
The cloning operation succeeds but t1 is excluded from the clone.
The cloning operation succeeds but t1 is cloned with current data.

explanation.correct: The operation fails because the historical data for table t1 at that point is no longer available in Time Travel (retention is 1 day, and the requested time is 2 days back).

distractors.A: t1's historical data is not available beyond 1 day.
distractors.C: The entire operation fails; it does not skip the table by default unless the IGNORE TABLES WITH INSUFFICIENT DATA RETENTION parameter is used.
distractors.D: It does not use current data; it fails.

questions.scenario: An administrator attempts to clone a database using Time Travel at a specific point in the past. Several conditions might cause the cloning operation to fail.
questions.stem: Select TWO scenarios that would cause the Time Travel cloning operation to fail.

options.text: The specified Time Travel time is beyond the retention time of any current child of the cloned database.
A pipe object with AUTO_INGEST = FALSE was recreated since the point in time specified.
A pipe object with AUTO_INGEST = TRUE was created after the point in time specified.
Any hybrid tables exist in the specified database and the IGNORE HYBRID TABLES parameter is not specified.
A pipe object with AUTO_INGEST = FALSE was dropped since the point in time specified.

explanation.correct: A is correct: the operation fails if the Time Travel time exceeds the retention period of any current child. D is correct: the operation fails if hybrid tables exist and IGNORE HYBRID TABLES is not specified.

distractors.B: This limitation applies only to pipes with AUTO_INGEST = TRUE, not FALSE.
distractors.C: Creation after the point in time is not a failure condition; only recreation or dropping of TRUE pipes since that time causes failure.
distractors.E: This limitation does not apply to pipes with AUTO_INGEST = FALSE.

questions.scenario: A table has a privacy domain with a REFERENCE privacy domain that references another table. Both tables are in the same schema. The schema is cloned.
questions.stem: Select TWO correct statements about the behavior of the REFERENCE privacy domain in the cloned objects.

options.text: If the schema is cloned, the new privacy-protected table references the new cloned version of the referenced table.
If the schema is cloned, the new table continues to reference the same referenced table as the source.
If the REFERENCE privacy domain references itself, the newly cloned table references the original table.
If you clone only the privacy-protected table (not the schema), the new table references nothing.
If you clone both the privacy-protected table and the referenced table, the new privacy-protected table references the new cloned version of the referenced table.

explanation.correct: A is correct: when the schema is cloned and both tables are included, the new privacy-protected table references the cloned referenced table. E is correct: if both tables are cloned together, the new table references the new cloned version.

distractors.B: If the schema is cloned, it references the cloned version, not the same original.
distractors.C: If the REFERENCE references itself, the newly cloned table references itself, not the original.
distractors.D: If you clone only the table, it continues to reference the original table, not nothing.

questions.scenario: A schema contains streams, tasks, and alerts. The schema is cloned.
questions.stem: Select THREE correct statements about the state of the cloned objects after the schema clone.

options.text: Unconsumed records in cloned streams are inaccessible.
Tasks in the clone are resumed by default.
Alerts in the clone are suspended by default.
Tasks in the clone are suspended by default.
Unconsumed records in cloned streams are fully accessible.
Alerts in the clone are resumed by default.

explanation.correct: A is correct: unconsumed records in cloned streams are inaccessible. C is correct: alerts are suspended by default. D is correct: tasks are suspended by default.

distractors.E: Records are inaccessible, not fully accessible.

questions.scenario: A table has the Search Optimization service enabled. The table is cloned.
questions.stem: Select TWO correct statements about the search access path in the cloned table.

options.text: The search access path in the clone is a zero-copy clone of the source.
The cloned search access path is always up-to-date and incurs no additional costs.
If the cloned search access path is not up-to-date, it may incur maintenance costs.
Search optimization is disabled on the cloned table and must be re-enabled.
The cloned search access path must be rebuilt from scratch.

explanation.correct: A is correct: the search access path in the clone is a zero-copy clone. C is correct: if it is not up-to-date, it may incur maintenance costs.

distractors.B: It may not be up-to-date and can incur costs.
distractors.D: Search optimization is not disabled; the access path is cloned.
distractors.E: It is a zero-copy clone, not rebuilt from scratch.

questions.scenario: A user plans to clone a large database while DML transactions are ongoing on source tables. They want to avoid failures due to data purging.
questions.stem: Select TWO recommended practices to prevent cloning failures in this situation.

options.text: Set DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 1 for all tables before starting the clone.
Set DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 0 for all tables to avoid storage costs.
Refrain from executing DML transactions on the source object until the clone completes.
Increase the warehouse size to speed up the clone operation.
Clone the database using Time Travel at an earlier point.

explanation.correct: A is correct: setting retention to 1 day ensures historical data is retained during the clone. C is correct: refraining from DML prevents data changes that could be purged.

distractors.B: Setting to 0 purges data quickly and can cause failures.
distractors.D: Warehouse size does not affect the data retention issue.
distractors.E: Using Time Travel does not prevent failures from ongoing DML; it may still fail if data is purged.
